narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 47% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1st out of 10—much lower than the typical student's 6th decile—and your Year 3 decile of 4th, which is also a bit below the average of around 5th or 6th. Your LSAT score of 24 stands out as notably lower than the dataset average of nearly 37, putting you in a challenging position compared to most applicants. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 is stronger than the typical 3.2, and your middle family income bracket aligns closely with averages, these didn't outweigh the other factors. Being a white female planning part-time study is common in the dataset—whites make up about 84% and females 44%, with part-timers rare at just 7%—but didn't shift the outcome much in your favor.

The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your Year 3 decile, which nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure since it was below average. Next in importance was your low LSAT score, which had a similar pushing effect toward failure. Your very low Year 1 decile ranked third and also contributed toward that riskier outlook. Family income came fourth, reinforcing the concern slightly, followed by your undergraduate GPA, racial background, and part-time status, all of which leaned toward predicting failure to varying degrees. Your gender as female was the least influential but uniquely worked in your favor, gently pulling the prediction toward bar passage success.

Overall, your profile shows strengths like a solid GPA but is held back by early and mid-school performance plus the LSAT, setting you apart from typical students who pass the bar with stronger showings in those areas. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 37, as that's a top factor and highly changeable. Building better study habits now could help if you pursue further education, potentially boosting later decile-like rankings. Switching to full-time study might also help, given how part-time paths correlate with higher failure risk in the data. With targeted prep, you could shift that 47% probability dramatically in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you've already shown GPA strength that proves your potential.
